summary:
The League of Nations, established in 1920 as part of the [[event:2644|Treaty of Versailles]] settlement, was the world's first permanent international organization dedicated to maintaining peace through collective security and diplomacy. It was the crowning achievement of American President Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points, embodying his vision of a new international order based on open diplomacy, national self-determination, and the peaceful resolution of disputes. Forty-two nations joined at its founding; membership eventually grew to 58.

The League's fundamental weakness was the absence of the United States. The US Senate refused to ratify the Versailles Treaty, and American isolationism kept the world's most powerful democracy outside the very institution its president had championed. The League's covenant required unanimous agreement in its Council for most decisions, and it had no standing military force of its own — enforcement depended entirely on member states' willingness to apply collective pressure or impose sanctions. These structural deficiencies were exposed repeatedly in the 1930s.

The League achieved genuine successes in its early years, resolving minor border disputes and establishing humanitarian agencies that became precursors to later UN bodies. But it failed its most important tests. Japan's invasion of Manchuria in 1931 and Italy's invasion of Ethiopia in 1935–36 exposed the organization's impotence: the major powers were unwilling to impose effective sanctions or take military action against aggressors. Germany, which had briefly joined, withdrew in 1933 after Hitler came to power. By the late 1930s the League was effectively moribund, unable to prevent the slide toward [[event:112|World War II]].

The League was formally dissolved in 1946, replaced by the [[event:2663|United Nations]], which was designed with lessons from its predecessor in mind — including giving the major powers veto rights in the Security Council to ensure they could not be outvoted into unwanted obligations, a compromise that traded idealism for great-power buy-in.
